title: Catechist's Guide — God is Father
html:
Catechist's Guide

For class preparation and delivery. For the catechist's use only.

Section 1 — Class Summary

Estimated duration: 60 minutes

Central theme: God reveals himself as Father in Scripture, and Jesus teaches us to relate to him with filial trust—not as a distant judge but as Abba, Daddy. This truth transforms how we understand prayer, sin, suffering, and our relationship with every other person.

Learning objectives:

  • Understand that God's name («I AM») reveals his being and his closeness.
  • Discover that God is Father from the Old Testament onward—including maternal images that enrich this truth.
  • Grasp the radical novelty of Jesus: God is Abba, Daddy, and we are invited into that intimacy.
  • Interiorize that «God is Love» (1 Jn 4:8) as the foundation of all Christian doctrine.
  • Live out filial trust as children of God, not as slaves to fear.
  • Approach the Our Father as Jesus' own gift—the prayer that places his words on our lips.
  • Understand how wounds from human fatherhood can be healed by divine fatherhood.

Key verse: «For you did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you received a spirit of adoption, through which we cry, 'Abba, Father!'» (Rom 8:15)

CCC references: 198-267; 2779-2785 (primary), plus 295-296 (creation) and 301-314 (providence and evil)

Section 2 — Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Big Question (Prodigal Son) 10 min Read or tell the parable from the father's perspective. Ask: «How do you imagine God receives you when you return to him?» Let the image sink in before moving to content.
2. God's Name: I AM 8 min Explain Ex 3:13-15. The name YHWH as presence, not merely a label. Connect the «I Am» of the burning bush to the «Abba» of Jesus—same God.
3. God the Father in the OT / Maternal Images 8 min Dt 32:6; Is 49:15; Ps 103:13; Hos 11:1-4. Show that God's fatherhood includes maternal tenderness. Read Hosea 11 aloud—it is deeply moving.
4. Jesus Reveals Abba / God is Love 10 min The radical intimacy of «Abba.» Read Mt 11:27; Jn 14:9; 1 Jn 4:8,16; 1 Jn 3:1. Help them feel the shock of calling God «Daddy» in first-century Jewish context.
5. The Parable as Portrait / Filial Trust 8 min Walk through each fatherly gesture. Introduce St. Thérèse's insight that God's justice IS mercy, because he knows us as a father knows his child. Read Rom 8:15 and Gal 4:6.
6. The Our Father / Providence / Evil 8 min The Our Father as Jesus' own prayer given to us. Briefly address Providence (Mt 6:25-30) and the problem of evil (point to the Cross as God's response).
7. For Reflection 6 min Ask participants to share one of the 5 reflection questions in small groups (2-3 people), then brief plenary sharing.
8. Closing: Key Idea + Prayer 5 min Read the Key Idea aloud. Pray the Our Father slowly together, emphasizing «Father» and pausing between phrases. Hand out the workbook.

Section 3 — Notes for the Catechist

The Parable of the Prodigal Son: Tell It from the Father's Perspective

This is the most important storytelling choice you will make. Most people know this story as «the prodigal son»—it's right there in the name. But Jesus' audience would have understood that the father is the true protagonist. Help your group notice each specific action: the father runs (undignified for an older man in that culture—he would have had to lift his tunic and expose his legs, a source of shame), embraces and kisses (gestures of tenderness that break through distance and protocol), restores dignity (robe, ring, sandals—each one carries symbolic weight; slaves went barefoot, sons did not; a ring signified authority), and throws a feast (the fattened calf was reserved for the greatest celebrations—this is total restoration). Don't forget the older son. Many in your group will identify with him—the dutiful one who feels unappreciated. The father's words to him are among the most beautiful in Scripture: «Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours» (Lk 15:31). Make sure participants hear that both sons are loved, both are invited.

The Name YHWH and the Word Abba

Explain that for Jews, the divine name YHWH was so sacred it was never pronounced—when reading Scripture aloud they substituted Adonai (Lord). This helps convey the reverence due to God. But Jesus introduces a word from the opposite end of the spectrum: Abba, the word a toddler uses for their father. It is almost impossible to overstate how shocking this was. A pious first-century Jew would not have dreamed of addressing the Creator of the universe with the intimacy of a child speaking to Daddy. Jesus doesn't just use this word himself—he teaches his disciples to use it. The reverence doesn't disappear; it's transformed into trust. This is the heart of Christian prayer.

«God is Love» (1 John 4:8,16)

This phrase is the interpretive key for all Christian doctrine. It is not one divine attribute among others; it is the very definition of who God is. God is not primarily power, justice, or wisdom (though he is these things too)—he is love. Everything else must be understood from this starting point. When people struggle with teachings about hell, judgment, or suffering, help them return to this foundation: whatever God does, he does as Love. If it doesn't look loving to us, the problem is our limited vision, not God's nature.

Filial Trust vs. Slavery to Fear

Many participants may carry a distorted image of God as a severe judge watching for their failures. This image often comes from harsh parenting, rigid religious formation, or projecting human authority figures onto God. Gently help them examine where their image of God comes from. Paul's language in Romans 8 is deliberately sharp: spirit of slavery vs. Spirit of adoption. The Christian life is not about fearful compliance but joyful trust. Ask: «If you knew God loves you unconditionally, what would change?»

The Problem of Evil and Suffering

Someone will almost certainly raise this. You don't need a definitive answer—no one has one. But you can point to the Cross as God's way of responding to evil: not with an explanation from above but with a presence from within. God doesn't eliminate suffering by divine decree; he enters it, takes it on himself, and transforms it from the inside. The question shifts from «Why does God allow suffering?» to «Where is God in suffering?» And the answer is: on the Cross, beside you, within you, carrying you. This is a Father who doesn't watch from a distance.

Wounds from Human Fatherhood

Be pastorally sensitive. For some people, the word «father» evokes pain, not comfort. Abuse, abandonment, coldness, impossible expectations—these are real wounds. The Catechism offers freedom here: God is neither male nor female (CCC 239). Divine fatherhood is not a bigger version of human fatherhood; it is the perfect reality of which all human fatherhood (and motherhood) is a faint copy. If someone struggles, suggest they begin with «Abba» (a fresh word with no painful associations) or with maternal images from Scripture (Is 49:15, Is 66:13). Healing is possible. Many saints—including St. Thérèse, who lost her mother at age four—found in God the Father precisely the healing their wounds required.

Section 4 — Age Adaptation

🌱 Seed (ages 7-12)

Use the metaphor of water and thirst: God is like a dad who gives us water when we're thirsty. Children at this age respond to concrete images and physical gestures. Activities: draw the burning bush with flames and Moses' sandals off, color the father embracing the son, memorize «God is Love» with hand signs (point up for God, cross arms over chest for love). Act out the Prodigal Son story with simple props—let them take turns being the father running to meet the son. The key takeaway for this age: «No matter what you do, God always loves you and is happy when you come back to him.» Avoid heavy discussion of suffering; for this age, focus entirely on the warmth of the father's welcome.

🌿 Sprouts (ages 13-17)

Connect with their experience of authority and identity. Teenagers are forming their own image of God, often in reaction to parents and authority figures. Ask gently: «Has a father figure disappointed you? How does that affect how you see God?» Use examples from movies or shows about fathers (e.g., Finding Nemo, The Lion King). The word «Abba» can help break the image of a distant, demanding God. Many teens feel they have to earn love through achievement; contrast this with the father in the parable who loves without conditions. The question «Which son are you?» often resonates powerfully—many teens feel like the older brother, dutiful but resentful. Give them space to be honest about anger toward God or parents without shutting it down.

🌱️ Roots (adults new to the faith)

Many adults carry wounds from their father figure. Be sensitive: not everyone has a good experience of fatherhood. Help them distinguish between human fatherhood (limited, sometimes failed) and divine fatherhood (perfect, unconditional). The testimony of converts who discovered God as Father can be extremely powerful—consider inviting a guest speaker or sharing a brief story from a saint or contemporary Catholic. Spend time on the distinction between slavery and adoption (Rom 8:15). New Catholics may have come from religious backgrounds that emphasized fear, rules, and earning God's favor. The Gospel's message that God loves first and loves freely is genuinely life-changing for them. Emphasize that God's fatherhood is not metaphorical—through baptism we truly become his adopted children, sharers in his divine life (CCC 1265).

🌲 Tree (formed adults)

Go deeper into the Trinitarian foundation: the Father loves the Son from all eternity, and that love is the Holy Spirit. God's fatherhood is not a human projection onto the divine; it is a theological reality revealed in Christ. Quote the Church Fathers: St. Augustine on the Father as Lover, the Son as Beloved, the Spirit as Love; St. Irenaeus on the Son and Spirit as the «two hands of the Father.» Explore Joseph Ratzinger's insight that the Christian God is not a solitary monad but a communion of Persons—a divine family into which we are adopted. Discuss how divine fatherhood illuminates the theology of grace: deification, filial adoption, participation in the divine nature (2 Pet 1:4). Engage difficult questions at a deeper level: theodicy, the relationship between divine justice and mercy, the Our Father as a summary of the whole Gospel (Tertullian called it the breviarium totius Evangelii). This is also a good group to read selections from Henri Nouwen's The Return of the Prodigal Son and discuss what it means to «become the father.»

Section 5 — Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 32-37 («What does it mean that God is Father?», «Why do we call God Father?»)
  • CCC paragraphs for deeper reading: 198-231 (God the Father), 239-241 (Father language and gender), 268-271 (omnipotence), 295-296 (creation), 301-314 (providence and evil), 2759-2785 (the Our Father)
  • Video: Bishop Robert Barron on the Prodigal Son (Word on Fire, available on YouTube). His commentary on the father's «running» is particularly powerful and accessible.
  • Music: «Good, Good Father» by Chris Tomlin (contemporary worship); «Abba Padre» by Hakuna Group Music (Spanish, but the melody is universal—also available in English versions).
  • Art: «The Return of the Prodigal Son» by Rembrandt (Hermitage Museum, St. Petersburg). Bring a printed image or project it. Guide participants to notice the two different hands of the father. Also: «The Creation of Adam» by Michelangelo (Sistine Chapel)—the space between the fingers as God's desire for relationship.
  • Book: Henri Nouwen, The Return of the Prodigal Son — a meditation on the parable and Rembrandt's painting that traces the three spiritual movements: younger son, older son, father. Accessible and deeply moving. Excellent for a parish book club.
  • Book: St. Thérèse of Lisieux, Story of a Soul — especially the early chapters on her childhood trust in God as Father and her discovery of the «Little Way.»
  • Film: The Mission (1986)—the final scene illustrating filial trust taken to its ultimate consequence: a love that does not defend itself because it trusts completely in the Father.
  • Papal catechesis: St. John Paul II's Wednesday audience series on «God is Father» (1980-1982). Available at vatican.va. Rich theological reflections accessible to the educated layperson.

Section 6 — Difficult Questions

«If God is an almighty Father, why does he allow innocent people to suffer?»

This is perhaps the hardest question of faith, and there is no answer that will satisfy the intellect completely—because we are touching a mystery. The Catechism offers several anchor points rather than a solution: (1) God does not cause evil—it enters through the misuse of created freedom (CCC 311). (2) God respects human freedom so profoundly that he permits even its misuse rather than turn us into puppets—and this permission is itself an act of love, because love cannot be forced (CCC 311). (3) God's power is shown not in preventing every evil but in bringing good out of evil—as a composer can transform a wrong note into a more beautiful melody (CCC 312). (4) The Cross is the definitive response: God doesn't explain suffering from a distance but enters it, shares it, and transforms it from within. The worst crime in human history—the murder of God's own Son—became the source of salvation for the whole world (CCC 313). In pastoral practice, don't try to «win» the intellectual argument. Instead, bear witness to the Cross. God does not remain silent in the face of suffering; he suffers with us and for us. That is what a Father does.

«Isn't it sexist to call God «Father»? Doesn't that imply God is male?»

God has no biological gender. The Catechism states this explicitly: God is «neither man nor woman» (CCC 239). The language of «Father» is not about anatomy but about relationship. It comes to us from Jesus himself—it is not a human invention projected onto God but a divine self-revelation. The Father is the origin, the source of life, the one who gives without receiving. Scripture also uses maternal imagery for God (Is 49:15, Is 66:13, Hos 11:3-4), showing that divine love embraces and surpasses what is best in both fatherhood and motherhood. Calling God «Father» is not about excluding women but about receiving the language Jesus gave us. Changing it would be changing how God chose to reveal himself. For participants struggling with this, you might add that the Hebrew word for mercy (rachamim) shares its root with the word for womb (rechem), suggesting that God's compassion is maternal in its depth even when we address him as Father.

«What if I had a bad father and I struggle to call God «Father»?»

This is a deeply understandable and pastorally significant question. The image of God as Father can be painful for those who have suffered abuse, abandonment, coldness, or indifference from their earthly father. The Church's teaching frees us here: divine fatherhood is not a magnified version of human fatherhood. It is the opposite. Divine fatherhood is the measure of all human fatherhood (Eph 3:14-15). Your human father was supposed to reflect God's fatherhood, imperfectly. If he failed, that does not tell you anything about God. God is unlike the father who hurt you. Practical helps: begin with «Abba»—it's a new word without painful associations. Use maternal images from Scripture (Is 49:15: «Can a mother forget her nursing child?»). Pray with Psalm 103, focusing on God's tenderness. Consider gently sharing a story of someone who found healing—Henri Nouwen's journey with Rembrandt's painting is a powerful example of a man who struggled with his own father wounds and found his way to the Father's embrace.

«Isn't the God of the Old Testament more of an angry judge than a loving Father?»

This is a common misunderstanding. The Old Testament is full of mercy: Hosea 11 portrays God as a parent teaching a toddler to walk, lifting him to his cheek. Psalm 103 describes a God who «does not deal with us according to our sins» and remembers that we are dust. The prophets again and again show God forgiving Israel after unfaithfulness. Conversely, the New Testament also contains judgment (Mt 25:31-46, the parables of the wedding feast and the talents). There is one God across both Testaments, and he reveals himself progressively (CCC 238). What changes is not God's character but the fullness of the revelation. In the Old Testament, God prepared his people through covenants, laws, and prophets. In Jesus, he gives himself. The Father we meet in Christ is the same God who spoke from the burning bush—fully revealed, fully given, fully love.

Section 7 — Opening and Closing Prayer Suggestions

Opening Prayer

Heavenly Father,
you who are the source of all love and all life,
send your Holy Spirit upon us as we gather.
Open our hearts to know you as you truly are:
not a distant judge but a Father who runs to meet us,
not a demanding master but Abba, Daddy,
who loves us before we deserve it
and who never stops waiting for our return.
Heal whatever in us resists trusting you.
Through Christ our Lord. Amen.

Closing Prayer — The Our Father (prayed slowly)

Invite the group to pray the Our Father together, but very slowly, pausing briefly after each phrase. Before beginning, say: «As we pray, remember that Jesus himself gave us these words. When we say 'Our Father,' we are stepping into his own relationship with the Father. We don't pray alone. We pray with Jesus, in Jesus, through Jesus. Let each word land.»

Section 8 — Catechist's Prayer

Father, you who are the source of all love,
thank you for calling me to serve you in catechesis.
Give me a father's heart for those who listen to me:
patience when they do not understand,
tenderness when they doubt,
firmness when they stray.
Help me never to project my own wounds or limitations
onto the image of you that I present.
May I not be an obstacle to their knowing you,
but a reflection of your loving fatherhood.
May those who see me
catch a glimpse of who you are:
a Father who runs, who embraces,
who celebrates the return of every child.
I place this class in your hands.
Do what I cannot do.
Touch hearts in ways I cannot reach.
Through Jesus Christ, your Son and our Brother. Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
